coredata.dc:title: Improved singlet oxygen generation and antimicrobial activity of sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots coupled with methylene blue for photodynamic therapy applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kholikov, Khomidkhodza
Ilhom, Saidjafarzoda
Sajjad, Muhammed
Smith, Michael E.
Monroe, Jerry D.
San, Omer
Er, Ali Oguz
coredata.dc:description: Due to their many unique properties, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention and are a promising material with potential applications in many fields. One application of GQDs is as a photodynamic therapy agent that generates singlet oxygen. In this work, GQDs were grown by focusing nanosecond laser pulses into benzene and then were later combined with methylene blue (MB) and used to eradicate the Gram-negative bacteria, Escherichia coli, and Gram-positive bacteria, Micrococcus luteus. Theoretical calculation of pressure evolution was calculated using the standard finite difference method. Detailed characterization was performed with transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR), UV–vis (UV–vis), and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Furthermore, MB-GQD singlet oxygen generation was investigated by measuring the rate of 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis(methylene) dimalonic acid photobleaching. Combining MB with GQDs caused enhanced singlet oxygen generation. Our results show that the MB-GQD combination efficiently destroys bacteria. The (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) (MTT) assay was used to determine if GQDs in dark conditions caused human cellular side-effects and affected cancer and noncancer cellular viability. We found that even high concentrations of GQDs do not alter viability under dark conditions. These results suggest that the MB-GQD combination is a promising form of photodynamic therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Methylene blue
Photodeactivation
Cell viability
Photodynamic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GQDs using electron microscopy
Characterization of GQDs using spectroscopy analysis
Stress wave simulation during GQD growth
Cytotoxicity measurements using the MTT assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Space-time distribution of the thermoelastic normal stress for pulsed laser power density
I 0
= 16 ×
10 8
W/cm2.
(a) TEM measurement of graphene quantum dots. The inset shows the lattice spacing of individual GQDs (the scale bar is 1 nm). (b) Size distribution of GQDs.
(a) UV–vis spectra and (b) photoluminescence of GQDs in DI water. Inset in (a) shows photostable fluorescent GQDs in DI and ethanol solutions.
FTIR spectra of a graphene quantum dot.
Singlet oxygen generation of MB-GQD at different ratios and time intervals.
(Left) E. coli (5 min irradiation) and (Right) M. Luteus colonies (2 min irradiation) in PBS before and after irradiation and their percentage irradiation of MB-GQD with 660 nm light.
Cell viability measurement of GQDs at various concentrations using the MTT assay on (A) IMR-90 and (B) A549 cell lines.

head.ce:title: Improved singlet oxygen generation and antimicrobial activity of sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots coupled with methylene blue for photodynamic therapy applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics & Astronomy, Western Kentucky University, Bowling Green, KY 42101, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Western Kentucky University, Bowling Green, KY 42101, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical & Aerospace Engineering, Oklahoma State University, Stillwater, OK 74078, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their many unique properties, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention and are a promising material with potential applications in many fields. One application of GQDs is as a photodynamic therapy agent that generates singlet oxygen. In this work, GQDs were grown by focusing nanosecond laser pulses into benzene and then were later combined with methylene blue (MB) and used to eradicate the Gram-negative bacteria, Escherichia coli, and Gram-positive bacteria, Micrococcus luteus. Theoretical calculation of pressure evolution was calculated using the standard finite difference method. Detailed characterization was performed with transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR), UV–vis (UV–vis), and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Furthermore, MB-GQD singlet oxygen generation was investigated by measuring the rate of 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis(methylene) dimalonic acid photobleaching. Combining MB with GQDs caused enhanced singlet oxygen generation. Our results show that the MB-GQD combination efficiently destroys bacteria. The (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) (MTT) assay was used to determine if GQDs in dark conditions caused human cellular side-effects and affected cancer and noncancer cellular viability. We found that even high concentrations of GQDs do not alter viability under dark conditions. These results suggest that the MB-GQD combination is a promising form of photodynamic therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a treatment that uses photosensitizing agents that, upon exposure to a certain wavelength of light, become toxic to targeted bacteria, malignant cells, and other diseased cells [1]. PDT is a frequently used alternative method to antibiotics and can eliminate antibiotic resistant pathogens [2–4]. In comparison to conventional methods, PDT shows fewer side effects, lower toxicity, and negligible development of drug resistance. The treatment of bacterial infections by PDT has been successfully applied in dermatology [5–7] and oncological diseases [8,9], where phenothiazine dyes are applied to the infected skin area and then illuminated with red light [10]. However, the current applications of organic PDT agents have drawbacks including poor water dispersibility, low photostability, and low absorption at high wavelengths [11,12]. Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), especially cadmium based QDs, have been introduced as an alternative to organic photosensitizers [11]. Despite their superior properties over standard photosensitizers, QDs exhibit low singlet oxygen quantum yield, cause cytotoxicity from the release of heavy metal ions (e.g., Cd2+), and can undergo degradation in cells, all of which impede their practical application [11,13]. Therefore, a biocompatible PDT agent that generates high singlet oxygen with high water dispersibility, excellent photostability and reduced cytotoxicity is needed.

A graphene quantum dot (GQD) is a single atom thick sheet of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms with nanoscale lateral dimensions less than 100 nm. GQDs integrate the unique properties of graphene inside the dot regardless of the dot size. Important properties of GQDs include low toxicity, high solubility in various solvents and the ability to have functional groups on their edges [14,15]. The cytotoxicity of GQDs has been found to be lower than that of their cousins, graphene oxides (GO), making them appropriate for biomedical applications [16,17]. In addition to their excellent thermal, chemical, and photoluminescent properties, graphene quantum dots can be effective photodynamic agents, producing high amounts of singlet oxygen when irradiated by visible light [14,18,19]. Graphene has a broad absorption spectrum between 400 and 700 nm and GQDs have a tunable non-zero bandgap which also varies by size and surface chemistry [14]. Excitons in graphene have an infinite Bohr radius, which makes the quantum confinement effect possible. GQDs exhibit size, shape, and edge dependent electrical properties. A complex interplay between size, shape, and edge states can govern GQD electrical properties [20].

Furthermore, GQDs were recently shown to exhibit a high 1O2 generation yield- greater than 1.3 via a new multistage sensitization process [19,21]. The presence of defects and free radicals at the surface of GQDs and direct energy transfer from GQDs to oxygen are believed to be responsible for singlet oxygen generation. In the conventional photosensitizer, singlet oxygen efficiency is known to be less than 1.0 and energy transfer is provided through an excited triplet state. During GQD excitation, in addition to conventional energy transfer from the triplet state, energy transfer from singlet to 3O2 may also lead to singlet oxygen generation during intersystem crossing from the singlet to triplet state.

GQDs killed U251 human glioma cells when irradiated with 470 nm blue light by causing oxidative stress [18]. The photodynamic activity of GQDs has also been investigated in HeLa cells and two strains of pathogenic bacteria, methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. Irradiation of GQDs caused HeLa cell shrinkage and bleb formation and killed the bacteria [19,22,23]. In addition, combining GQDs with low doses of H2O2 was effective against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria by increasing OH− generation [24].

In this work, we have used laser ablation to synthesize GQDs for use in the photodeactivation of different strains of bacteria. GQDs were characterized by TEM, SEM, FTIR, UV–vis spectroscopy, and PL spectra. Thermoelastic expansion of liquid benzene, a source material for graphene quantum dots, was induced by surface heating. The stress field of this expansion was simulated using a standard finite difference method. GQDs were later mixed with methylene blue (MB) to increase singlet oxygen generation and photoresponsivity. We show that the use of MB-GQDs enhances singlet oxygen generation and destroys Gram-negative and -positive bacteria colonies within 10 min of irradiation. We also found that these new GQDs do not have evident side-effects in human cells as they are not cytotoxic to a cancer (A549) and non-cancer (IMR-90) cell line.

Graphene quantum dots were synthesized with a pulsed Nd: YAG laser (Continuum Surelite II, 1064 nm wavelength, 10 Hz frequency, and 5 ns pulse width). Nickel (II) oxide powder of was mixed with benzene and was irradiated with a focused laser beam while being stirred with magnetic bars. Laser energy density was changed in the range of 210–1050 mJ/cm2 and the samples were irradiated for varying times of 30, 45, and 60 min. After irradiation, the sample containing NiO, benzene, and graphene quantum dots was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant substance was collected and rotary evaporated to separate benzene from the graphene quantum dots. The GQDs were then dissolved in deionized (DI) water, sonicated, and centrifuged once more at 11,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant part was collected and filtered via syringe filters.

It was recently reported that the sulphur-doped GQDs have improved electronic properties and surface chemical reactivities compared to bare GQDs and also exhibit higher quantum confinement [25,26]. Therefore, we also prepared sulphur-doped GQDs by the same method, with the exception of adding only 50 mg of sulfur to the initial NiO and benzene mixture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs using electron microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and microstructure of GQDs were investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) by placing a droplet of the suspension on a 400-mesh lacey copper grid. Similarly, a droplet of GQD suspension was dried on a carbon tape on an aluminum stub and was analyzed by scanning electron microscope electron diffraction spectroscopy (SEM-EDS) operating at the secondary electron imaging (SEI) mode at 20 kV to find the elemental composition percentages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs using spectroscopy analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate chemical structure and bond formation, attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy was performed. A UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to analyze the absorbance of the GQD solutions and to record the rate of 9, 10-anthracenediyl-bis (methylene) dimalonic acid (ABMDMA) photobleaching at 400 nm [27]. The photoluminescence spectra of GQDs was recorded using a spectrofluorometer.

The rate of singlet oxygen generation was measured by determining the level of photobleaching of ABMDMA at 400 nm. In each trial, 60 μl of ABMDMA (final concentration being ∼0.07 mg/ml) and 25 μl of the stock solution MB (6.2 * 10−5 M) was used. Then, 100:1, 10:1, and 1:1 ratios of MB to sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots (SGQDs) (stock solution conc. 1 mg/mL) were mixed with ABMDMA. The samples were irradiated with 660 nm red light from a light emitting diode of 12 W from a 9 cm height for 5 min.

The antibacterial effect of MB-GQDs was tested using the Gram-negative bacteria, Escherichia coli, and a Gram-positive bacteria, Micrococcus luteus. Bacteria were grown in LB broth at 37 °C for 24 h for E.coli and 48 h for M. luteus. The number of colony forming units per mL (CFU/ml) was determined using a hemocytometer. A concentration of ∼ 106 CFU/ml was used. Four different samples each having a separate control were prepared as depicted in Fig. 1 . Samples consist of (1) bacteria only, (2) bacteria + GQD, (3) bacteria + MB, and (4) bacteria + MB:GQD. One sample was irradiated with red LED light for 5 min and the other was not exposed to serve as a control.

After irradiation, the samples were serially diluted and plated on LB agar media and incubated at 37 °C. The number of colonies in each bacterial sample was counted after 24 and 48 h intervals. The experiment was conducted in a phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.4) in the dark to prevent photodeactivation and all samples were tested in triplicate.

Cell lines were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection. The A549 lung cancer cell line was grown in F12 K media with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin supplementation. The IMR-90 lung fibroblast cell line was grown in Eagle’s Minimum Essential Medium with supplementation as in A549 and 0.25 μg/ml of amphotericin B. Both cell lines were incubated at 37 °C in 5% CO2 with passaging every 3–4 days.

The 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) cellular viability assay was performed in both cell lines. Cells were seeded at 10,000 cells (A549) and 5000 cells (IMR-90) per well in 96-well plates and incubated at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for 24 h [28]. Then, wells were treated in replicates of eight according to a concentration series (200, 150, 100, 50, 25 μg/mL) of GQDs for 24 h. A negative control (media only), positive control (Triton X-100) and series of blanks were simultaneously performed. The assay was run for 2 h, then the wells were rapidly evacuated and treated with solubilization solution [10% Triton X-100 in acidic (0.1 N HCl) isopropanol] for 15 min before the plates were read using a BioTek Synergy HT microplate reader (570 nm and 690 nm). GraphPad Prism v6 was used to perform statistical analysis and represent the data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stress wave simulation during GQD growth
ce:section.ce:para: When a laser beam is focused in to liquid media, it creates an ablation plume which contains species such as atoms, ions, and clusters, traveling with high kinetic energy. Liquid media acts as the confinement medium for plasma, and the plasma expands adiabatically at supersonic velocity creating a shock wave in front of it. Confining plasma with liquid media affects the thermodynamic and kinetic properties of plasma plume evolution. The plasma produced in this manner has a higher temperature compared to the plasma created under ambient pressure. Due to the intensity of the laser pulses and short pulse width, the temperature and pressure within the reaction volume can be extreme (many thousands of K and few GPa) [29,30].

Given the importance of pressure during laser-matter interactions for nanoparticle growth, we used finite difference analysis to estimate the peak pressure when liquid benzene is irradiated by nanosecond laser pulses. Further details of the numerical method utilized in this study can be found elsewhere [31–33] using the material properties and optical constants of liquid benzene [34–36]. Fig. 2 illustrates the space-time profiles of normal stress caused by pulsed-laser irradiation of liquid benzene. Our simulations show a peak pressure of about 2 GPa and 2500 K maximum temperature when using a power density of
I 0
= 16 ×
10 8
W/cm2. It should be noted that these values are for the unfocused beam and will be higher when the laser energy density is increased.

Fig. 3 shows the TEM image of the graphene quantum dots after 45 min of irradiation with 750 mJ/cm2 laser pulses. HR-TEM results show that particles ranging from 2 to 7 nm in size and regular shape were formed. The statistical analysis indicated that the distribution is similar to a Gaussian distribution.

The average size is about 3.8 nm with narrow size distribution. The measured lattice distribution is 0.24 nm, corresponding to 1120 interlayer spacing of graphene [37]. When sulphur was added to our solutions, we did not notice any changes in either the TEM, UV–vis, or PL measurement. However, the EDS with SEM analysis showed only trace amounts of sulphur compared to GQD only samples.

UV-vis spectroscopy and photoluminescent emission were employed to investigate the optical properties of the GQDs. Fig. 4 shows the UV–vis measurement of GQDs in water with absorption peaks around 267 and 340 nm. These bands are likely to arise from oxygen related surface states which correspond to the n-π* transition states of the CO bond [38]. It is known that emission of GQDs are strongly dependent on pH, solvent, spin, and excitation wavelength [39]. GQDs were dissolved in DI water and ethanol to a concentration of 4 mg/ml. The DI water solution looks pale yellow while the ethanol solution looks dark yellow. Fluorescence was readily seen in both samples when they were irradiated by 365 nm UV light. The different color of these samples is probably due to solvent attachment or different emissive traps on the surface of the GQDs [40].

All of the obtained GQDs show excitation-and solvent- dependent photoluminescence. The PL emission of GQDs shifts from 420 to 445 nm as the excitation wavelength increases from 310 to 350 nm. GQDs emit visible light at different wavelengths depending on the excitation wavelength. The maximum PL emission occurred at 420 nm, when it was excited at 310 nm with a Stokes shift of 110 nm a similar dependence of PL emission on the excitation wavelength has been reported [38,40–42]. The GQDs demonstrated excellent photostability. Even after two months they still showed extraordinary fluorescence.
ATR-FTIR is a powerful sampling technique used in combination with IR spectroscopy which allows an easy analysis of solid and liquid samples without further preparation.

The ATR-FTIR spectra in Fig. 5 shows the bands of different GQD functional groups. The broad peak at 3330 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching of the hydrogen bond in hydroxyl (OH) groups. The two sharp peaks at 1720 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1 represent stretching vibrations from carbonyl (CO) and epoxide functional groups. The band at 1600 cm−1 corresponds to (CC) skeletal stretching vibrations from graphitic domains. Lastly, a (COH) stretching band was observed at 1225 cm−1.

Our results show that as the concentration of GQDs increases, the ABMDMA band decreases faster, hence singlet oxygen generation increases, as shown in Fig. 6 . The MB-GQD solution photobleaches faster than ABMDMA compared to pristine MB, indicating a higher amount of singlet oxygen generation efficiency. Photosensitizers used with heavy atoms were previously shown to enhance 1O2 generation efficiency [43]. The incorporation of carbonyl groups into a polymer matrix can also significantly enhance singlet oxygen (1O2) generation and suppress production of other reactive oxygen species [44]. Enhanced two-photon excitation singlet oxygen generation efficiency in the nanoparticles was attributed to efficient intraparticle energy transfer in photosensitizer-doped conjugated polymer nanoparticles [45]. It should be noted the two photon absorption is a nonlinear process where two low frequency photons with high intensity are absorbed. Since two photon absorption quadratically depends on laser fluence, a careful adjustment is needed to avoid laser-induced bacterial damage [46].

It is known that the efficiency of singlet oxygen generation is proportional to the population of the excited triplet photosensitizers, which are produced by intersystem crossing (ISC) from the excited singlet photosensitizers. Therefore, the GQDs probably increase the lifetime of the triplet state of methylene blue and increase the efficiency of ISC from singlet-MB to triplet-MB.

Water soluble cationic phenothiazinium compounds such as methylene blue not only have poor cell/tissue penetration and accumulation, they also have low biological stability in free molecular form as they are rapidly reduced to an inactive form, leuco methylene blue. In addition, MB has the disadvantage of inherent dark toxicity. Thus, MB’s use in in vivo PDT with cancer has been limited. Nanocomplexed MB has greatly enhanced cell internalization while keeping the high photosensitization efficiency of free MB, leading to a distinct phototoxicity toward cancer cells [47]. In an effort to increase therapeutic efficiency and reduce unwanted side effects, hybrid photosensitizers consisting of nanoparticles and photosensitizing agents have been developed [48–52]. Our results show that GQD, as a biocompatible PDT agent, can be used in combination with MB while increasing singlet oxygen generation with reduced side effects and without the dark toxicity of MB.

Our experiments indicate that 106 CFU/ml Gram-negative E. coli and Gram positive M. Luteus can be eradicated entirely in 10 min with MB-GQD irradiation. Fig. 7 shows the results after irradiating E. coli for 5 min and M. luteus for 2 min.

Our results also show that Gram-positive bacteria are deactivated faster compared to Gram-negative bacteria. When MB-GQD is used, the deactivation rate improved more than twice compared to samples with MB only within 2 min in M. luteus. The higher improvement rate was observed in E.coli with 5 min of irradiation. We have found that Gram-negative bacteria are more difficult to inactivate than Gram-positive bacteria due to their negatively charged outer membrane, which covers a single thin peptidoglycan layer between cell mebrane and outer membrane layer [53–55]. When the same concentration of Gram-positive bacteria was irradiated with MB-GQD, no M. luteus colonies were present after 5 min. This could be due to enhanced singlet oxygen generation from interaction between GQD and MB in addition to GQD disruption of the bacterial cell envelope. When GQDs are used with MB, it is possible that the GQDs may cause membrane disruption, and generating channels large enough to allow MB to enter cytoplasm. Once MB enters the cell, it generates singlet oxygen and prevents protein synthesis and DNA replication. This interpretation is supported by our singlet oxygen generation results where higher amount of singlet oxygen is produced when the ratio of MB to GQD is 1:1 as shown in Fig. 6. GQDs may also interact with MB in other ways including dimer formation and in the form of electrostatic force [56]. Also, in addition to increased transition from intersystem crossing between the singlet and triplet states, an increased gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) due to interaction between MB and GQDs is also possible. When MB, silver nitrate, and silver nanoparticles are combined to react together against bacteria, the effects can be up to three orders of magnitude greater than the sum of the two components acting alone against bacteria [57]. It should be noted that there was no significant decrease in the viability of the bacteria cells when treated with GQD only under both conditions; with and without 660 nm irradiation. The red light itself has no effect on bacteria viability as well. In addition, samples containing bacteria and MB without irradiation showed no antibacterial activity within the irradiation duration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity measurements using the MTT assay
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs can be innately cytotoxic depending on their concentration, size, and photo exposure time [22,58]. Many studies have shown that GQDs are well tolerated at low concentrations. For example, 100% cell viability was observed when the concentration of GQDs was less than 50 μg/ml but nearly 50% of the cells died at a concentration of 1 mg/ml [58–61]. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of our GQDs, cell viability was measured using the MTT assay.

As shown in Fig. 8 , cells cultured with GQDs possessed almost the same viability as untreated cells under dark conditions, indicating the high biocompatibility of GQDs in the absence of light, even with 200 μg/ml. In fact, at 200 μg/ml, cell viability increased in the A549 cell line. Increased cell viability at 200 μg/ml could be attributed to an interaction between GQDs and membrane proteins that modulate and/or activate signal transduction mechanisms involved with cellular proliferation and migration. It is unlikely that the GQDs can modulate cellular functions by entering the cell and targeting physiological mechanisms. The GQDs used in this study have a minimum size of 2 nm. Thus, they should be too large to diffuse across the cell membrane and through membrane protein channels [62,63]. However, we cannot rule out GQD cellular uptake via endocytotic or similar mechanisms.

In summary, graphene quantum dots were grown using nanosecond laser pulses. Surface characterization tools were used to determine their electronic properties and the crystal structure of GQDs. The average GQD size obtained was 3.8 nm. GQDs exhibited excellent photostability and biocompatibility. When GQDs were combined with MB, singlet oxygen generation increased. Gram-positive and -negative bacteria treated with combined MB-GQD were deactivated within 5 min of being irradiated. The GQDs did not reduce the cellular viability of either cancer or non-cancer human cell lines. MB used in PDT must be limited due to its inherent dark toxicity and low stability under reductive biological conditions. Our results suggest that when sulphur-doped GQDs are combined with MB, a lower amount of MB will produce more anti-bacterial activity. MB-GQDs could be effective in killing bacterial pathogens in open wounds, skin cancers, tumors located near the skin and in plasma purification. Further studies are required to understand the exact mechanism of MB-GQDs and to fully use their potential. For example, two photon absorption could be used instead of single photon. Given that GQDs produce singlet oxygen at the lower portion of the visible spectra, we believe that two photon excitation of MB-GQDs will cause less cellular damage and deeper light penetration in vivo compared to single photon irradiation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Dr John Andersland for TEM and SEM measurement and Ms Naomi Rowland for helping on bacteria culturing. This project is fully supported by Kentucky Biomedical Research Infrastructure Network and INBRE (KBRIN) ULRF13-1493C-01.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pdpdt.2018.08.011.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
